perturbation.original_text: Attendee shall notify BO MI immediately upon discovery of unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information or any breach of this Agreement, and shall cooperate with BOMI in every reasonable way to assist BOMI in mitigating the consequences of such use or disclosure and preven ting further unauthorized use or disclosu re.
perturbation.changed_text: Attendee shall notify BO MI within 30 days of discovery of unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information or any breach of this Agreement, and shall cooperate with BOMI in every reasonable way to assist BOMI in mitigating the consequences of such use or disclosure and preven ting further unauthorized use or disclosu re.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notification timeline from 'immediately' to 'within 30 days' introduces a delay that could potentially violate regulations requiring prompt reporting of data breaches.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) Breach Notification Rule
perturbation.law_explanation: HIPAA requires covered entities to notify affected individuals, HHS, and, in some cases, the media, of a breach of unsecured protected health information without unreasonable delay, and in no case later than 60 calendar days from the discovery of the breach. While this NDA is not directly related to HIPAA, similar breach notification regulations in other industries may require faster notification than 30 days in cases of sensitive personal data compromise. The modified text could be interpreted as conflicting with the promptness requirement of such regulations by unduly delaying breach reporting.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Breach Notification Rule | HHS.gov Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock A locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Navigate to: Breach Notification Rule The HIPAA Breach Notification Rule, 45 CFR §§ 164.400-414, requires HIPAA covered entities and their business associates to provide notification following a breach of unsecured protected health information. Similar breach notification provisions implemented and enforced by the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) , apply to vendors of personal health records and their third party service providers, pursuant to section 13407 of the HITECH Act. Definition of Breach A breach is, generally, an impermissible use or disclosure under the Privacy Rule that compromises the security or privacy of the protected health information. An impermissible use or disclosure of protected health information is presumed to be a breach unless the covered entity or business associate, as applicable, demonstrates that there is a low probability that the protected health information has been compromised based on a risk assessment of at least the following factors: The nature and extent of the protected health information involved, including the types of identifiers and the likelihood of re-identification; The unauthorized person who used the protected health information or to whom the disclosure was made; Whether the protected health information was actually acquired or viewed; and The extent to which the risk to the protected health information has been mitigated. Covered entities and business associates, where applicable, have discretion to provide the required breach notifications following an impermissible use or disclosure without performing a risk assessment to determine the probability that the protected health information has been compromised. There are three exceptions to the definition of “breach.” The first exception applies to the unintentional acquisition, access, or use of protected health information by a workforce member or person acting under the authority of a covered entity or business associate, if such acquisition, access, or use was made in good faith and within the scope of authority. The second exception applies
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the notification timeline from 'immediately' to 'within 30 days' of discovering unauthorized use or disclosure. The Perturbation Explanation highlights the potential violation of regulations requiring prompt reporting of data breaches. The Law Explanation further elaborates that similar breach notification regulations may require faster notification than 30 days, potentially conflicting with the modified text. Scraped Law Snippet 2 from HHS.gov states that the HIPAA Breach Notification Rule, 45 CFR §§ 164.400-414, requires HIPAA covered entities and their business associates to provide notification following a breach of unsecured protected health information. While the contract isn't explicitly under HIPAA, the principle of prompt notification is a key aspect of data breach regulations. Therefore, changing 'immediately' to 'within 30 days' contradicts the intent of prompt notification requirements, supporting a 'YES' determination.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any litigation between the Parties in connection with this Agreement, including the seeking of injunctive relief, if BOMI is the successful party, Attendee shall pay to BOMI therein all co sts and expenses, including but not limited to actual attorneys’ fees incurred therein by BOMI, which costs, expens es and attorneys’ fees shall be included as a 2 part of any judgment rendered in such action in addition to a ny other relief to which BOMI may be entitled. Should BOMI not be a successfu l party, the Parties will each bear their own attorneys’ fees and expenses.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of any litigation between the Parties in connection with this Agreement, including the seeking of injunctive relief, the prevailing party shall not be entitled to recover attorneys' fees and costs from the non-prevailing party, regardless of the outcome of the litigation. Should BOMI not be a successfu l party, the Parties will each bear their own attorneys’ fees and expenses.
perturbation.explanation: Disallowing the prevailing party to recover attorneys' fees can conflict with Maryland law, which may allow for fee shifting in certain contract disputes or specific types of claims.
perturbation.law_explanation: Maryland Rule 2-704 governs attorney’s fees. In a breach of contract case where the contract allows recovery of attorney's fees to the prevailing party, this blanket prohibition may contradict contract law principles of enforcing contractual agreements. The modification contradicts the ability of the prevailing party (BOMI) to recover attorney's fees, especially if the original agreement implicitly or explicitly provides for such recovery.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Message - Maryland Code and Court Rules This document is not available on Westlaw.
perturbation.justification: The modification directly contradicts the original agreement's provision for the prevailing party to recover attorneys' fees, especially if the original agreement implicitly or explicitly provides for such recovery. The changed text removes a pre-existing right, replacing it with a blanket prohibition. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is unavailable, the Law Explanation emphasizes that in a breach of contract case, if the contract allows recovery of attorney's fees to the prevailing party, this blanket prohibition may contradict contract law principles of enforcing contractual agreements. This aligns with the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition, where the changed text creates a term violating state law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by and c onstrued according to the laws of the State of Maryland (excluding the choice of laws ru les thereof). Attendee hereby consents to the jurisdiction of the courts of the State of Maryland. Any su it brought hereon must be brought in the state or federal courts sitti ng in the State of Maryland, the parties hereto hereby waive any claim or defense that such foru m is not convenient or proper.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by and c onstrued according to the laws of the State of California (excluding the choice of laws ru les thereof). Attendee hereby consents to the jurisdiction of the courts of the State of California. Any su it brought hereon must be brought in the state or federal courts sitti ng in the State of California, the parties hereto hereby waive any claim or defense that such foru m is not convenient or proper.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Maryland to California might conflict with the enforceability of the forum selection clause and the overall agreement if the attendee has no connection to California.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 410.30 allows a court to stay or dismiss an action when it finds that in the interest of substantial justice the action should be heard in a forum outside California. Changing the governing law to California when the Attendee and BOMI International (based in Maryland) have no connection to California, could lead a California court to determine that California is an inconvenient forum, rendering the forum selection clause unenforceable and potentially invalidating the agreement. Additionally, some California laws might conflict with the agreement's original intent under Maryland law, particularly regarding attorney's fees, as shown in the perturbation directly above.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 410.30 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.30. (a) When a court upon motion of a party or its own motion finds that in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state, the court shall stay or dismiss the action in whole or in part on any conditions that may be just. (b) The provisions of Section 418.10 do not apply to a motion to stay or dismiss the action by a defendant who has made a general appearance. (Amended by Stats. 1972, Ch. 601. Note: This version was suspended from Sept. 22, 1986, until Jan. 1, 1992, during operation of the temporary amendment by Stats. 1986, Ch. 968.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the agreement's governing law and forum from Maryland to California. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this change may cause enforceability issues if the attendee lacks a connection to California. The Law Explanation supports this, citing California Code of Civil Procedure § 410.30, which allows a court to dismiss an action if California is an inconvenient forum. Scraped Law Snippet 1 directly quotes CCP 410.30(a), stating that a court can stay or dismiss an action if 'in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state'. Therefore, changing the forum and governing law to California, when no connection to California exists, directly contradicts the ability to enforce the forum selection clause under California law.

perturbation.original_text: Attendee agrees that money damages w ill not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by him and that in addition to all other remedies which may be available, BOMI will be entitled to specific perf ormance and injunctive or other equitable relief, without bond, as a remedy for any such breach or threatened breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Attendee agrees that money damages w ill not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by him and that in addition to all other remedies which may be available, BOMI will be entitled to specific perf ormance and injunctive or other equitable relief, with bond, as a remedy for any such breach or threatened breach.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring a bond for injunctive relief, while seemingly minor, may contradict established Maryland law principles governing when bonds are required for injunctions.
perturbation.law_explanation: Maryland Rule 15-504 addresses security requirements for injunctions. While the rule does not explicitly prohibit waiving a bond, case law and equitable principles often guide a judge's discretion on whether a bond is necessary to protect the enjoined party from harm if the injunction is later found to be wrongfully issued. By mandating a bond in all cases, the modified text removes the court's discretionary power to waive the bond requirement where it is deemed unnecessary or inappropriate, potentially conflicting with established equitable practices.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Message - Maryland Code and Court Rules This document is not available on Westlaw.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed for injunctive relief "without bond", while the changed text requires it "with bond". The Law Explanation states that Maryland Rule 15-504 and associated case law allows a judge discretion on whether a bond is required. By mandating a bond in all cases, the modified text removes the court's discretionary power and potentially conflicts with established equitable practices. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is unavailable, the Law Explanation strongly suggests the modified text contradicts established legal principles by removing judicial discretion related to bond requirements for injunctive relief.
